title: Chapter 10: The Laughter
content: The dining room of Château Thornfield held fourteen people and a lie so elegant it wore silk.

Candlelight caught the rim of Jane's champagne flute as she turned toward the Japanese retailer across the table — a compact man with impeccable posture who'd flown from Osaka that morning and still looked sharper than everyone in the room. She was discussing sustainability metrics in the Asian market, and she was doing it brilliantly, the numbers flowing from her tongue like she'd been born to this. The black Thornfield blazer she wore moved with her gestures, the signature brooch at her collar catching light — a small silver thing, a thorn and a thread, the house's emblem. She'd pinned it there herself. It felt like armor. It felt like a costume.
At the head of the table, Edward held court.

He was magnificent tonight. There was no other word for it. He stood at the end of the long oak table, one hand on the back of his chair, the other cutting through the air as he spoke, and every person in the room watched him the way moths watch flame — with the knowledge that the heat might burn them and the absolute inability to look away. He was telling the story of Thornfield. The real story, he said, his voice low and warm, carrying the way great voices do — not through volume but through gravity. How his grandmother had sewn her first coat in a bombed-out atelier in postwar Lyon. How the house had survived three recessions, two hostile takeovers, and one catastrophic collection in 1987 that had almost ended everything. He paused for effect. The investors laughed. The editors leaned forward.
"And yet," Edward said, spreading his hands, "here we are."

Here we are. Jane sipped her champagne. The bubbles rose against her palate, fine and precise, the taste of money made drinkable. The wine was a 2012 Dom Pérignon, because of course it was. Everything at this table was curated — the Baccarat glassware, the Limoges plates, the arrangements of white peonies that perfumed the air with something almost funereal in its sweetness. The food came in courses: foie gras, then a lobster bisque, then a rack of lamb so perfectly medium-rare it looked like it had died willing. The conversation flowed through fashion and finance, through the upcoming shows and the new markets, through the shape of the future as seen from a château that had survived revolution and occupation and was now surviving something else entirely.

Jane performed her role. She was good at roles. She asked the right questions of the right people, laughed at the right moments, touched Edward's arm once — just once — when he made a joke about Italian fabric merchants, a gesture so natural it might have been rehearsed for weeks. And perhaps it had been. She couldn't tell anymore where the performance ended and she began. Perhaps that was the point.

Between the bisque and the lamb, there was a pause. The staff cleared plates with the invisible efficiency of people who had been trained not to exist, and the conversation fractured into smaller, private exchanges. Someone was debating the merits of Milan versus London Fashion Week. Someone else was texting under the table, the glow of the screen visible against the white tablecloth. The room hummed with the comfortable noise of powerful people eating expensive food in someone else's house.
And then Jane heard it.
Laughter.

Not the bright, performative laughter from around the table — she knew that sound, had produced it herself all evening. This was different. This came from below. From under the stone floor, from somewhere beneath the foundation of the château, rising through the ancient walls like steam through a crack. It was wild laughter, bitter and brilliant, the kind that starts in the chest and tears its way out through the throat. It had edges. It had intelligence. It was the laughter of someone who had once been magnificent and knew it, and knew what had been taken from her, and found the whole arrangement cosmically, gut-wrenchingly funny.
It lasted perhaps five seconds. Then it faded, like a hand withdrawing from a wound.
Jane's hand had frozen on her champagne flute. She could feel the crystal against her fingers, cold and precise, and she could feel her own pulse in her fingertips. She looked around the table.
No one had reacted.

The investors were still talking. The editors were still smiling. The Japanese retailer was still composing something on his phone. No one had paused mid-sentence. No one had glanced toward the floor. No one's expression had shifted by even a millimeter. It was as if the sound had never happened — or as if every person in this room had been trained, through some unspoken curriculum, not to hear it.
Jane set down her champagne. The bubbles had gone flat.
Edward caught her expression.

Of course he did. He didn't miss anything. She'd learned that about him in six days — the way his attention was a searchlight that never fully dimmed, that swept the room even when he seemed most absorbed in conversation. His eyes found hers across the table, and something moved behind them. Recognition. Alertness. The quick calculation of a man who was always, always listening for a particular sound.

He rose from his seat and moved down the table toward her with the easy territorial grace of a man who owned the room and everything in it, including the air. He leaned in, one hand on the back of her chair, close enough that she could smell his cologne — cedar and black pepper and something underneath that was just him, warm and animal. His voice was low, conspiratorial, the voice he used to share secrets and make you feel like you were the only person in the world who mattered.
"The old plumbing." A half-smile. "This château has a mind of its own."

He laughed, soft and indulgent, as if inviting her to share in the charming eccentricity of old houses and their noises. His hand brushed her shoulder — reassuring, possessive, both at once. Then he straightened and returned to his end of the table, reclaiming the conversation with a remark about the Loire Valley's infrastructure that made everyone chuckle.

The whole performance had taken four seconds. Maybe five. It was practiced. That was the word that lodged in Jane's chest like a splinter. Practiced. The speed of the redirect, the warmth of the deflection, the casual perfection of the timing — he had done this before. He was always listening for that sound. He was always ready.
The woman to Jane's left — an editor from Paris, silver-haired, deliberately unadorned — murmured without looking up from her wine: "Every old house has its ghosts."

Her eyes lifted, briefly, to meet Jane's. And in that glance, Jane saw it: the knowledge. This woman knew it wasn't plumbing. She knew it wasn't the house settling or the pipes groaning or any of the comfortable fictions that the wealthy told themselves in old buildings. She knew. And she was choosing — deliberately, with the practiced ease of someone who had made this choice many times before — to look away.
Jane's lamb arrived. She ate it without tasting.

The main course ended. Coffee was offered. The conversation circled like water around a drain, and the drain was always the same: the upcoming Paris Fashion Week show. Thornfield had the closing slot — the final presentation of the week, the one that everyone would remember because it was the last thing they saw. The IPO announcement would follow. The numbers were, apparently, extraordinary. Everyone at this table stood to make a great deal of money, and the anticipation was a living thing in the room, electric and feral beneath the polished conversation.
Jane excused herself.

"I'm just going to find the powder room," she said to no one in particular, and no one in particular nodded, and she walked away from the table with the unhurried steps of someone who needed only the bathroom, nothing more.

The corridor was cool after the warmth of the dining room. Tapestries lined the walls — hunting scenes, mythological figures, the usual iconography of French aristocratic decoration. Jane walked past them without looking. Her heels were quiet on the stone floor, though not silent; she understood, instinctively, that silence would be more suspicious than noise. Normal people made sound. Normal people walked to the bathroom and walked back.
She wasn't going to the bathroom.

The corridor curved left, then right, then opened into a narrower passage that she recognized from her earlier explorations — the one that led toward the service stair. The overhead lights were period-appropriate, brass sconces with low-wattage bulbs that cast more shadow than illumination. The stone walls seemed to breathe.
The laughter had stopped. The silence was total, so complete it felt deliberate, as if the house itself was holding its breath. Jane rounded the final corner and saw the door.
It was wrong.

Everything else in this corridor — the stone walls, the wood-beamed ceiling, the antique sconces — belonged to the eighteenth century. The door did not. It was heavy steel, industrial gray, set into the ancient stone frame with visible bolts and a rubber seal along the threshold. The lock was electronic: a small, glowing keypad with a numeric code, the kind of system that logged entries and required a PIN. It hummed faintly, a sound you'd only notice in absolute silence.

Someone had spent real money on this door. Real money and real deliberation. This wasn't a retrofit or an afterthought — it was an installation, planned and executed with purpose. In a château where every original detail had been preserved with almost religious devotion, this door was a confession in steel and circuitry. Something down there was worth locking in.
Jane reached out and touched it.

The metal was cold — not cool, cold, the deep cold of something that led underground, where stone and earth conspired against warmth. Her fingertips pressed against the surface, and she felt the tiny vibration of the electronic lock, the hum of current maintaining the barrier. She pressed her ear to the door.
Nothing. No sound from below. No laughter, no footsteps, no scissors. Just the blank, sealed silence of a room that had been designed to contain whatever was inside it.

She stepped back. Her reflection stared at her from the steel surface — distorted, rippled, barely human. The brooch at her collar caught the faint light of the corridor and threw it back, a small bright point in the dark.
She walked back to the dinner party.

The conversation had moved on to dessert — a construction of chocolate and gold leaf that was either a pastry or a sculpture, Jane couldn't tell. Champagne was poured again, the pop of the cork like a tiny gunshot. Toasts were proposed: to Thornfield, to Paris, to the future. Jane raised her glass. She smiled. She laughed at someone's joke. She touched Edward's hand when he reached for her across the table, and his fingers closed around hers, warm and sure, and he looked at her with such perfect, calculated affection that she almost — almost — believed it herself.

But under the tablecloth, out of sight, her other hand had made a fist. And in her mind, behind the performance, she was cataloguing: electronic lock, steel door, rubber seal. The cost of containment. The architecture of silence.

The dinner ended. Guests departed in waves — handshakes and air kisses and the particular French formality of departure that took longer than the arrival. Jane stood beside Edward at the château's entrance, the great doors open to the Loire night, and said goodbye to every one of them. Her cheeks ached from smiling. The last car pulled away at midnight, its taillights red points vanishing down the drive, and then there was only the night and the château and the silence.
Edward kissed her forehead. "You were perfect tonight," he said. "They loved you."
Then he went to his study, and Jane climbed the stairs to her room alone.

She sat on the edge of the bed and opened her notebook. The pages were filling up — days of observations, small truths tucked between lines of administrative notes, a parallel record that no one knew she was keeping. She uncapped her pen.

*A house full of people who hear a woman laughing from the basement and pretend it's plumbing. That's not discretion. That's conspiracy. Every person in that room chose not to hear her. What would it take for them to choose otherwise? What would it take for me?*
She stared at what she'd written. Then, below it: *The door cost more than most people's apartments. You don't spend that kind of money on plumbing.*

She capped the pen. Set the notebook on the nightstand. Lay back on the bed and stared at the ceiling — old plaster, painted white, a network of fine cracks like the surface of an egg. The château was quiet now. The guests were gone. The staff had retreated to their quarters. Even Edward's study light had clicked off.
And then, from below — faint, so faint she might have imagined it — the sound of scissors.

She'd heard them before. Late at night, the snick-snick of blades cutting fabric, rhythmic and deliberate. She'd assumed it was the atelier — a dedicated sewer working late, a last fitting for the show. But the atelier was on the ground floor, east wing. This sound came from below. From the basement. From behind the steel door with the electronic lock.
Jane held her breath.

The scissors paused. Then began again — but differently now. Not the steady, unconscious rhythm of someone lost in work. This was deliberate. A pattern. Three short cuts, a pause, one long cut. Three short. Pause. One long. Three short. Pause. One long.
SOS.

The rhythm repeated, precise and unmistakable, and Jane's hand went to her chest, to the brooch she hadn't removed, the thorn and the thread, and she felt her heartbeat against the silver like a fist against a door.
The scissors were a conversation now. Someone down there knew she could hear. Someone down there was speaking to her.
And she was listening.
